_comment: Probe manifest (docs/02 §4): the editable knob that drives `update-offsets` offset
re-discovery. It describes what is provably TRUE at the fixed calibration setup — practice
mode, P1 Jin vs P2 Kazuya, round start — so calibration after a patch is a DATA EDIT here,
not a source change. Everything here is FACTS/DATA (known values, plausibility bounds, scan
windows), not copyrightable expression (docs/02 §5). Extra keys like this comment are ignored.
The scan windows are module-relative placeholders aligned to the C4a placeholder table; they
WILL need calibration on the real build (widen/relocate, or set `absolute` and point at an
address you located — player structs behind a pointer chain are not reachable by a
module-relative window). See the runbook printed by `update-offsets`.
notes: Default manifest — calibrate scan windows on the real build (docs/02 §4).
_base_scan_comment: C4d code-signature base derivation (docs/02 §3): locate the HEAP-allocated player struct by
scanning the module's static data for the pointer that leads to it, follow the pointer chain,
and validate the landing against the known within-struct field layout (the ORACLE). All values
here are FACTS/DATA (docs/02 §5): the within-struct field offsets and the stable-ish chain shape
were SEEDED from the community-known Tekken 8 layout (dcep93/TekkenBot's committed
memory_address.ini) — NOT the fork's update_memory_address.py script. `base_offset` (the static
slot) is NOT seeded: it shifts every build and is re-derived by the scan.
`pointer_path` = module+base_offset -> deref +0x10 -> +0x68 -> +0x8 -> +0x30. round_start_health
is full HP (200, confirmed live). The struct has no direct HP field, so health is computed as
round_start_health - damage_taken (the fork's model). struct_span is the in-struct health/position
scan bound; it is capped to the derived stride at runtime, so setting it >= max_stride just means
'scan the whole player struct'. These are durable; the runbook covers what to recalibrate.
`state_fields` (C4e): Tekken 8 stores ENCODED state words, not the per-flag booleans our schema
assumed. These offsets are facts/data from the same layout source; their decimal values are
move_frame 0x370, counter_state (hit_outcome) 0x5f0, recovery_state 0x5b4, simple_move_state
0x640, stun_type 0x644, throw_tech_state 0x668, complex_move_state 0x68c. The scalar KINDS are
hypotheses (u32 for the 4-aligned words) — calibrate if a field reads implausibly. The VALUE
semantics live in state-map.json and are calibrated by observation (docs/02 §8): no round-start
two-snapshot oracle can prove what stun_type==3 means, because nobody is in stun at round start.
The layout also carries attack_type at 0x4f1 (u8, high/mid/low); PlayerFrame has no home for it,
so it is deliberately not read.
`legacy_state_fields` are the C4a placeholder's per-flag booleans. They describe a struct that
does not exist on the real build, so the derivation DROPS them from the table it writes rather
than carrying forward offsets that would look like they work.
`component_scan` (C4e): position is NOT in the entity struct — a full-struct scan across a
walking snapshot pair finds no moving float triple, and the layout data has no position field.
It lives in a separate Unreal transform component the entity points at, so the scan sweeps the
entity's own pointer slots and looks for the moving (x,y,z) triple inside the pointee.
_global_scan_comment: C4e global/match anchor derivation (docs/02 §3). The global struct is heap-allocated behind its
own static pointer, exactly like the player struct, so the anchor is re-derived per build. What
is seeded is FACTS/DATA: the chain shape and the within-struct offsets known to carry match
state. The layout source records the frame counter as the offset run
but does NOT say where the pointer chain ends and the field offsets begin. So `pointer_paths` is
a LIST of candidate chain shapes, tried longest-first; the behavioral oracle rejects the wrong
ones, which is exactly what makes guessing here safe. [0] and [] cover the degenerate cases (a
single deref, and a global struct sitting directly at the slot).
to carry frame counter, round, timer and phase, but not in a known order. The scan assigns them
BY BEHAVIOR across two snapshots — one ticks up, one holds a round number, one counts down —
which is both clean-room and robust to the source data being reordered. Practice mode usually
freezes the round clock, so timer_ms is often unassignable; frame_counter + round is what the
oracle requires. Add offsets here rather than assigning them by hand.
The layout source also carries a static `opponent_side` at 0x9ad7060 +0x4. Static offsets like
that shift every build (that is why base_offset is re-derived), so it is recorded here as a fact
